nodes.text: The FAIR Principles define characteristics that contemporary data resources, tools, and infrastructures should exhibit. To be Findable: data are assigned globally unique persistent identifiers and registered in searchable resources. To be Accessible: data are retrievable via standardized protocols; metadata remain accessible even when data are unavailable. To be Interoperable: data use shared languages for knowledge representation and formal vocabularies. To be Reusable: data are richly described with provenance, usage licenses, and domain-relevant standards.

nodes.text: The existing digital ecosystem surrounding scholarly data publication prevents extraction of maximum benefit from research investments. Good data management is essential not as an end itself but as the key conduit to knowledge discovery and innovation. What constitutes good data management remains largely undefined and left to data owners and repositories. Funding agencies and publishers increasingly require data management plans, recognizing that data must be discoverable and reusable for downstream investigation. The ecosystem fragments between special-purpose repositories such as GenBank and UniProt, which excel at capturing curated reference datasets for both human and mechanical users, and general-purpose repositories—Dataverse, FigShare, Dryad, Zenodo—that accept diverse data types with minimal integration or harmonization. The resulting ecosystem moves away from centralization, becoming more diverse and less integrated, exacerbating discovery and reusability problems.

nodes.text: Consider a researcher with a dataset of differentially-selected polyadenylation sites in a pathogenic organism under infection conditions. Comparing these to other examples requires hunting across repositories: Are the desired metadata—species, tissue, data type, conditions, genes—captured, searchable, and in usable formats? Can disparate datasets be integrated automatically without error? What are the licensing conditions and citation requirements? Gathering such integrative data often requires weeks or months of specialist effort. The reason is not lack of technology but that valuable digital objects are not paid the careful attention they deserve when created and preserved. The goal is for scholarly digital objects to become first-class citizens in scientific publication, where impact derives from ability to be found, reused, and cited by all stakeholders—human and computational.

nodes.text: This recognition prompted a 2014 workshop in Leiden, "Jointly Designing a Data Fairport," bringing together diverse academic and private stakeholders. Four foundational principles emerged: Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability, and Reusability—FAIR. The principles apply to all research objects: data, algorithms, tools, and workflows. Distinct from initiatives focused on human scholarship, FAIR emphasizes enhancing the ability of machines to automatically find and use data. Humans and machines face distinct barriers when discovering and processing data. Humans identify contextual cues and infer meaning but operate at limited scope and speed. Machines must autonomously act when encountering diverse types, formats, and access mechanisms. Machine-actionability exists on a spectrum: a machine may determine data-type but lack parsers for unknown formats; it may process data but not determine licensing requirements. Bespoke parsers for each data-type across all programming languages and analytical tools are unsustainable. FAIR focuses on assisting machines through generalized interoperability technologies and standards at the data and repository level.

nodes.text: The four FAIR principles comprise specific requirements: Findable objects are assigned globally unique persistent identifiers and described with rich metadata; Accessible objects are retrievable by identifier using standardized protocols, with metadata remaining accessible when data are unavailable; Interoperable objects use formal, accessible, shared languages for knowledge representation; Reusable objects are richly described with relevant attributes, released with clear usage licenses, detailed provenance, and domain standards. FAIR differs from related initiatives in describing concise, domain-independent, high-level principles applicable to diverse scholarly outputs. The principles are modular and can be adopted incrementally as environments evolve toward greater FAIRness. Repositories including Dataverse, UniProt, and wwPDB already implement aspects of FAIR using varied technologies. The broader community coalesces around a shared vision spanning the scholarly data ecosystem. All data producers and publishers are called to examine and implement these principles and actively participate in the FAIR initiative, advancing the critical goals of data stewardship and knowledge discovery.

nodes.text: Good data management is not a goal in itself, but the key conduit to knowledge discovery and innovation, and to data and knowledge integration and reuse by the community after publication. The existing digital ecosystem surrounding scholarly data publication prevents extracting maximum benefit from research investments. Science funders, publishers, and government agencies increasingly require data management and stewardship plans for publicly funded experiments. Beyond collection, annotation, and archival, stewardship includes long-term care of valuable digital assets so they can be discovered and reused in downstream investigations, either alone or in combination with newly generated data. Good data management produces high-quality digital publications that facilitate and simplify the ongoing process of discovery, evaluation, and reuse. Yet what constitutes good data management remains largely undefined, generally left to data and repository owners. Defining clear goals and desiderata would help those who publish and preserve scholarly data navigate these obstacles and maximize the added-value gained by contemporary formal scholarly digital publishing.

All scholarly digital research objects benefit from their application, since all components of the research process must be available to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and reuse.

nodes.text: Numerous and diverse stakeholders benefit from overcoming these obstacles: researchers wanting to share, receive credit, and reuse data and interpretations; professional data publishers; software and tool builders providing analysis and processing services; funding agencies concerned with long-term stewardship; and data scientists mining and analyzing new and existing data for discovery. Humans, however, are not the only critical stakeholders in the scientific data ecosystem. Applications and computational agents, increasingly tasked with data retrieval and analysis on behalf of humans, are also vital—and their importance grows. One of the grand challenges of data-intensive science is to improve knowledge discovery by assisting both human scholars and computational agents in finding, accessing, integrating, and analyzing task-appropriate scientific data and other scholarly digital objects.

nodes.text: For certain data types, well-curated special-purpose repositories exist—GenBank, the Worldwide Protein Data Bank, and UniProt in life sciences; the Space Physics Data Facility and astronomical databases in space sciences. These foundational and critical core resources continuously curate high-value datasets, fine-tune them for scholarly output, support both human and mechanical users, and provide extensive rich tooling for access. Not all datasets fit these models, however. Traditional bench science produces important datasets that do not fit the data models of special-purpose repositories, yet remain equally important for integrative research, reproducibility, and general reuse.

nodes.text: In response, numerous general-purpose repositories have emerged at scales ranging from institutional to globally open—Dataverse, FigShare, Dryad, Mendeley Data, Zenodo, DataHub, DANS, and EUDat. These repositories accept diverse data types in varied formats, generally do not attempt to integrate or harmonize deposits, and place few restrictions on deposition descriptors. The resulting ecosystem moves away from centralization, becomes more diverse and less integrated, thereby exacerbating discovery and reusability problems for both human and computational stakeholders.

nodes.text: Consider a specific example: a researcher has generated a dataset of differentially-selected polyadenylation sites in a non-model pathogenic organism grown under environmental conditions that stimulate its pathogenic state. She wants to compare these alternatively-polyadenylated genes to other examples of alternative-polyadenylation and to expression levels of these genes in related organisms during the infection process. With no special-purpose archive for polyadenylation data and no model organism database for this pathogen, where does she begin? If such datasets existed and were published, where might they be found? The desired search would need to filter for specific species, tissues, data types, conditions, and genes. Is such metadata captured by repositories? In what formats? Is it searchable? Once data is discovered, can it be downloaded? In what formats? Can that format be integrated with private in-house data and other third-party data publications and community repositories? Can this be done automatically to avoid errors? What are the license conditions? Who should be cited if a data-point is reused?

nodes.text: These barriers to discovery and reuse, particularly acute for machines, represent the kinds of deeply integrative analyses that constitute contemporary data-intensive science. The reason months of specialist effort are often needed to gather data for such research questions is not lack of appropriate technology. Rather, valuable digital objects do not receive the careful attention they deserve during creation and preservation. Overcoming these barriers requires all stakeholders—researchers, special-purpose repositories, and general-purpose repositories—to evolve to meet emergent challenges. The goal is for scholarly digital objects to become first-class citizens in the scientific publication ecosystem, where publication quality and impact are functions of their ability to be accurately found, reused, and cited over time, by all stakeholders, human and mechanical.

nodes.text: To realize this goal, a workshop was held in Leiden, Netherlands, in 2014, named "Jointly Designing a Data Fairport." It brought together a wide group of academic and private stakeholders all interested in overcoming data discovery and reuse obstacles. From deliberations emerged the notion that through defining and widely supporting a minimal set of community-agreed guiding principles, all stakeholders could more easily discover, access, appropriately integrate and reuse, and adequately cite vast quantities of information generated by contemporary data-intensive science. The workshop concluded with draft foundational principles—that all research objects should be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable (FAIR) for machines and people. A dedicated working group subsequently refined these principles.

nodes.text: Humans and machines often face distinct barriers when attempting to find and process data. Humans possess intuitive semantics—they are capable of identifying and interpreting a wide variety of contextual cues, whether in page layout, visual design, or narrative notes—making them less prone to selection errors when sufficient metadata exists. However, humans cannot operate at the scope, scale, and speed required by contemporary scientific data and data-intensive science. Computational agents increasingly undertake discovery and integration tasks on behalf of humans, necessitating machines to autonomously and appropriately act when faced with diverse types, formats, and access mechanisms. The agents must also maintain exquisite provenance records so collected data can be accurately and adequately cited.

nodes.text: Throughout this work, "machine actionable" indicates a continuum where a digital object provides increasingly detailed information to autonomous computational explorers. This information enables the agent—to a degree dependent on detail provided—to identify the type of object, determine usefulness by interrogating metadata and data elements, assess usability regarding license and accessibility constraints, and take appropriate action, much as a human would. A machine may determine data-type but not parse unknown formats; it may process contained data but not determine licensing requirements. The optimal state—where machines fully understand and autonomously operate on a digital object—may rarely be achieved. Nevertheless, the FAIR principles provide steps along a path toward machine-actionability; adopting them, in whole or part, moves the resource along the continuum.

Creating bespoke parsers in all languages for all data-types and all analytical tools is not a sustainable activity. Thus, focus on assisting machines through generalized interoperability technologies and standards at data and repository levels becomes first-priority for good data stewardship.

nodes.text: The FAIR Guiding Principles describe distinct considerations for contemporary data publishing environments supporting manual and automated deposition, exploration, sharing, and reuse. While recent domain-focused publications advocate specific improvements, FAIR differs in describing concise, domain-independent, high-level principles applicable to diverse scholarly outputs. The elements are related but independent and separable, defining characteristics contemporary data resources, tools, vocabularies, and infrastructures should exhibit to assist discovery and reuse. By minimally defining each principle, the barrier-to-entry for producers, publishers, and stewards wishing to make holdings FAIR remains intentionally low. The principles may be adhered to in any combination and incrementally as providers' environments evolve toward increasing FAIRness. Their modularity explicitly supports wide-ranging special circumstances, including sensitive or personally-identifiable data, where rich metadata facilitating discovery and clear access rules provide high FAIRness even without FAIR publication of the data itself.

nodes.text: The broader community of stakeholders is coalescing around common, dovetailed visions spanning all facets of the scholarly data publishing ecosystem. Implementation will yield more rigorous management and stewardship of these valuable digital resources, benefiting the entire academic community. Good data management and stewardship is not an end in itself, but a precondition supporting knowledge discovery and innovation. Contemporary data-intensive science requires data to be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable over the long term. These objectives are rapidly becoming expectations of agencies and publishers. The FAIR Guiding Principles provide mileposts for data producers and publishers, guiding implementation of fundamental good data management and stewardship practice and helping researchers adhere to funding agency expectations and requirements.

nodes.text: There is an urgent need to improve infrastructure supporting scholarly data reuse. A diverse set of stakeholders—academia, industry, funding agencies, and publishers—have designed and endorsed the FAIR Data Principles: a concise, measurable set of guidelines for enhancing data reusability. Unlike peer initiatives focusing on human scholars, FAIR emphasizes enhancing machines' ability to automatically find and use data, in addition to supporting human reuse.

nodes.text: Good data management is the key conduit to knowledge discovery and innovation. The existing digital ecosystem surrounding scholarly data publication prevents us from extracting maximum benefit from our research investments. Science funders, publishers, and governmental agencies increasingly require data management and stewardship plans for publicly funded research. Beyond proper collection, annotation, and archival, data stewardship includes long-term care of valuable digital assets, discovered and reused for downstream investigations. The outcomes are high-quality digital publications facilitating discovery, evaluation, and reuse in subsequent studies. What constitutes good data management is, however, largely undefined and generally left as a decision for the data or repository owner. Bringing clarity around the goals of good data management and defining guideposts to inform those who publish and preserve scholarly data would be of great utility.

nodes.text: Four foundational principles—Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability, and Reusability—serve to guide data producers and publishers as they navigate these obstacles. These principles apply not only to data in the conventional sense, but also to the algorithms, tools, and workflows that led to that data. All scholarly digital research objects—from data to analytical pipelines—benefit from application of these principles, since all components of the research process must be available to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and reusability.

nodes.text: One of the grand challenges of data-intensive science is to improve knowledge discovery through assisting both humans and their computational agents in discovering, accessing, integrating, and analyzing task-appropriate scientific data and scholarly digital objects. For certain types of important digital objects, there are well-curated, deeply-integrated, special-purpose repositories such as Genbank, Worldwide Protein Data Bank, and UniProt in the life sciences. These foundational core resources continuously curate and capture high-value reference datasets, providing extensive tooling to access their content. However, not all datasets fit the data models of special-purpose repositories.

nodes.text: These questions highlight barriers to data discovery and reuse—not only for humans, but even more for machines, yet precisely these integrative analyses constitute contemporary e-Science. The reason we often need weeks or months of specialist technical effort to gather necessary research data is not lack of appropriate technology. Rather, we do not pay our valuable digital objects the careful attention they deserve when we create and preserve them. Overcoming these barriers requires that all stakeholders—researchers, special-purpose and general-purpose repositories—evolve to meet emergent challenges. The goal is for scholarly digital objects of all kinds to become first-class citizens in the scientific publication ecosystem, where publication quality and impact are functions of its ability to be accurately and appropriately found, reused, and cited over time by all stakeholders, both human and mechanical.

nodes.text: With this goal in mind, a workshop was held in Leiden, Netherlands, in 2014, named "Jointly Designing a Data Fairport." This workshop brought together a wide group of academic and private stakeholders interested in overcoming data discovery and reuse obstacles. From the deliberations emerged the notion that, through the definition of and widespread support for a minimal set of community-agreed guiding principles and practices, all stakeholders could more easily discover, access, appropriately integrate and reuse, and adequately cite the vast quantities of information generated by contemporary data-intensive science. The meeting concluded with foundational principles—that all research objects should be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) both for machines and for people. A dedicated FAIR working group subsequently fine-tuned and improved the Principles.

nodes.text: Humans and machines often face distinct barriers when attempting to find and process data on the Web. Humans have an intuitive sense of semantics—the meaning or intent of a digital object—because we are capable of identifying and interpreting a wide variety of contextual cues, whether structural, visual, or narrative. We are less likely to make errors in selecting appropriate data, although humans face similar difficulties if sufficient contextual metadata is lacking. The primary human limitation is that we are unable to operate at the scope, scale, and speed necessitated by contemporary scientific data and e-Science complexity. Humans increasingly rely on computational agents to undertake discovery and integration tasks on their behalf.

nodes.text: Machine-actionable indicates a continuum of possible states wherein a digital object provides increasingly detailed information to an autonomously-acting computational data explorer. This information enables the agent—to a degree dependent on detail provided—to identify the type of object with respect to both structure and intent; determine if it is useful within the agent's current task; determine if it is usable with respect to license and accessibility constraints; and take appropriate action as a human would. A machine may determine the data-type of a discovered digital object but not be capable of parsing it due to unknown format, or be capable of processing data but not capable of determining licensing requirements. The optimal state—where machines fully understand and can autonomously operate on a digital object—may rarely be achieved. Nevertheless, the FAIR principles provide steps along a path toward machine-actionability; adopting them, in whole or in part, leads the resource toward this optimal state.

nodes.text: Machine-actionability applies in two contexts: first, referring to contextual metadata surrounding a digital object (what is it?), and second, referring to the content of the digital object itself (how do I process it?). Either or both may be machine-actionable. We must distinguish between data that is machine-actionable through specific investment in software supporting that data-type—bespoke parsers—and data that is machine-actionable exclusively through general-purpose, open technologies. Ultimate machine-actionability occurs when a machine can make a useful decision regarding data it has not encountered before. This distinction matters when considering the rapidly growing data environment with new technologies continuously being developed, and the growth of general-purpose repositories where data-types likely to be encountered are unpredictable. Creating bespoke parsers in all computer languages for all data-types and analytical tools is not a sustainable activity. The focus on assisting machines through more generalized interoperability technologies and standards at the data and repository level becomes a first priority for good data stewardship.

nodes.text: Representatives of stakeholder groups coalesced around four core desiderata—the FAIR Guiding Principles. To be Findable: (meta)data are assigned a globally unique and persistent identifier; data are described with rich metadata; metadata clearly and explicitly include the identifier of the data it describes; (meta)data are registered or indexed in a searchable resource. To be Accessible: (meta)data are retrievable by their identifier using a standardized communications protocol that is open, free, and universally implementable, with allowance for authentication and authorization where necessary; metadata are accessible even when data are no longer available. To be Interoperable: (meta)data use a formal, accessible, shared, and broadly applicable language for knowledge representation; (meta)data use vocabularies that follow FAIR principles; (meta)data include qualified references to other (meta)data. To be Reusable: (meta)data are richly described with accurate and relevant attributes; (meta)data are released with a clear and accessible data usage license; (meta)data are associated with detailed provenance; (meta)data meet domain-relevant community standards.

nodes.text: These high-level FAIR Guiding Principles precede implementation choices and do not suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation solution. They are not themselves a standard or specification. They act as guides to data publishers and stewards to evaluate whether their particular implementation choices render their digital research artifacts Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable. We anticipate that these principles will enable a broad range of integrative and exploratory behaviors based on diverse technology choices and implementations.

nodes.text: FAIR differs from other data management initiatives in that it describes concise, domain-independent, high-level principles applicable to a wide range of scholarly outputs. The elements are related but independent and separable. They define characteristics that contemporary data resources, tools, vocabularies, and infrastructures should exhibit to assist discovery and reuse by third parties. By minimally defining each guiding principle, the barrier to entry for data producers, publishers, and stewards wishing to make their data holdings FAIR is purposely maintained as low as possible. The Principles may be adhered to in any combination and incrementally as publishing environments evolve toward increasing degrees of FAIRness. Their modularity explicitly supports a wide range of special circumstances—such as highly sensitive data, where publication of rich metadata facilitating discovery provides high FAIRness even without FAIR publication of the data itself.

nodes.text: The Principles apply equally to non-data research objects. Analytical workflows are critical components of the scholarly ecosystem, and their formal publication is necessary to achieve transparency and scientific reproducibility. Diverse repositories demonstrate FAIR in practice. Dataverse, an open-source data repository software installed in dozens of institutions globally, generates formal citations for each deposit, makes Digital Object Identifiers public upon publication, resolving to landing pages providing access to metadata, data files, terms, and licenses—all indexed and searchable. Metadata is always public even if data are restricted or removed for privacy. FAIRDOM integrates platforms for Systems Biology with research assets identified by unique persistent URLs registerable with DOIs, accessible in multiple formats appropriate for individuals and computational agents. Open PHACTS provides a machine-accessible interface for drug discovery information offering both human and machine readable representations. wwPDB hosts experimentally-determined 3D structures of proteins and nucleic acids, with entries stably hosted in machine-readable formats represented using Macromolecular Information Framework standards, enabling fully-automated retrieval. UniProt provides comprehensive protein sequence and annotation data, with all entries uniquely identified by stable URLs offering access in multiple formats, utilizing shared vocabularies and ontologies with explicit typing leaving no ambiguity about what data represents.

nodes.text: The ideas within the FAIR Guiding Principles reflect, combine, and build upon previous work by the Concept Web Alliance, who focused on machine-actionability and data harmonization, and by organizations that developed the Joint Declaration of Data Citation Principles, who focused on making scholarly data citable, discoverable, and reusable. FAIR complements the Data Seal of Approval in that both aim to render data reusable for users other than those who originally generated them. The broader community of stakeholders is coalescing around a set of common, dovetailed visions spanning all facets of the scholarly data publishing ecosystem. Good data management and stewardship is not a goal in itself, but rather a precondition supporting knowledge discovery and innovation. Contemporary e-Science requires data to be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable in the long-term, and these objectives are rapidly becoming expectations of agencies and publishers. The FAIR Data Principles provide mileposts for data producers and publishers, guiding the implementation of basic levels of good data management and stewardship practice. We call on all data producers and publishers to examine and implement these principles and actively participate with the FAIR initiative. By working together toward shared, common goals, the valuable data produced by our community will gradually achieve the critical goals of FAIRness.

nodes.text: There is an urgent need to improve the infrastructure supporting the reuse of scholarly data. A diverse set of stakeholders—representing academia, industry, funding agencies, and scholarly publishers—have come together to design and jointly endorse a concise set of principles referred to as the FAIR Data Principles. These may act as a guideline for those wishing to enhance the reusability of their data holdings. Distinct from peer initiatives that focus on the human scholar, the FAIR Principles put specific emphasis on enhancing the ability of machines to automatically find and use the data, in addition to supporting its reuse by individuals. This Comment includes the rationale behind them and exemplar implementations in the community.

nodes.text: Good data management is not a goal in itself, but rather the key conduit leading to knowledge discovery and innovation, and to subsequent data and knowledge integration and reuse. Unfortunately, the existing digital ecosystem surrounding scholarly data publication prevents us from extracting maximum benefit from our research investments. Science funders, publishers and governmental agencies are beginning to require data management and stewardship plans. Beyond proper collection, annotation, and archival, data stewardship includes the notion of 'long-term care' of valuable digital assets, with the goal that they should be discovered and re-used for downstream investigations. The outcomes from good data management and stewardship are high quality digital publications that facilitate discovery, evaluation, and reuse in downstream studies. What constitutes 'good data management' is, however, largely undefined, and is generally left as a decision for the data or repository owner. This article describes four foundational principles—Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability, and Reusability—that serve to guide data producers and publishers as they navigate these obstacles and maximize the value gained by contemporary scholarly digital publishing. Importantly, the principles apply not only to 'data' in the conventional sense, but also to the algorithms, tools, and workflows that led to that data. All scholarly digital research objects—from data to analytical pipelines—benefit from application of these principles, since all components of the research process must be available to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and reusability.

nodes.text: Numerous and diverse stakeholders stand to benefit from overcoming these obstacles: researchers wanting to share, get credit, and reuse each other's data and interpretations; professional data publishers; software and tool-builders providing analysis and processing services; funding agencies increasingly concerned with long-term data stewardship; and the data science community mining, integrating and analysing data to advance discovery. Humans, however, are not the only critical stakeholders. Similar problems are encountered by the applications and computational agents that we task to undertake data retrieval and analysis on our behalf. These 'computational stakeholders' are increasingly relevant and demand as much, or more, attention as their importance grows. One of the grand challenges of data-intensive science is to improve knowledge discovery through assisting both humans and their computational agents in the discovery of, access to, and integration and analysis of task-appropriate scientific data and scholarly digital objects. For certain types of important digital objects, there are well-curated, deeply-integrated, special-purpose repositories such as Genbank, Worldwide Protein Data Bank (wwPDB), and UniProt in the life sciences; Space Physics Data Facility and Set of Identifications, Measurements and Bibliography for Astronomical Data in the space sciences. These foundational core resources continuously curate high-value reference datasets and provide support and tooling for both human and mechanical users. However, not all datasets can be captured by these repositories. Many important datasets from traditional bench science don't fit the data models of special-purpose repositories, yet are equally important for integrative research, reproducibility, and reuse. In response, numerous general-purpose data repositories have emerged at scales ranging from institutional to globally-scoped—Dataverse, FigShare, Dryad, Mendeley Data, Zenodo, DataHub, DANS, and EUDat. These repositories accept a wide range of data types in various formats, generally do not integrate or harmonize deposited data, and place few restrictions on descriptors. The resulting ecosystem appears to be moving away from centralization, becoming more diverse and less integrated, thereby exacerbating discovery and re-usability problems for both human and computational stakeholders.

nodes.text: A specific example illustrates these obstacles. Suppose a researcher has generated a dataset of differentially-selected polyadenylation sites in a non-model organism grown under various environmental conditions. The researcher is interested in comparing the alternatively-polyadenylated genes in this dataset to other examples of alternative-polyadenylation and the expression levels of these genes—both in this organism and related model organisms—during infection. Given that there is no special-purpose archive for differential polyadenylation data and no model organism database for this pathogen, where does the researcher begin? If the desired datasets existed, where would they be published, and how would one search for them? The desired search would need to filter by specific species, tissues, data types, conditions, and genes—is this metadata captured by repositories, in what formats, searchable how? Once discovered, can data be downloaded and in what formats? Can that format be integrated with private in-house data and third-party publications? Can integration happen automatically, avoiding copy/paste errors? What license conditions apply, and who should be cited if a data-point is re-used? Such questions highlight barriers to data discovery and reuse, particularly for machines; yet these deeply integrative analyses constitute contemporary e-Science. The reason we often need weeks or months of specialist technical effort to gather data for such research questions is not the lack of appropriate technology. The reason is that we do not pay our valuable digital objects the careful attention they deserve when we create and preserve them. Overcoming these barriers requires that all stakeholders—researchers, special-purpose and general-purpose repositories—evolve to meet emergent challenges. The goal is for scholarly digital objects of all kinds to become 'first class citizens' in the scientific publication ecosystem, where quality and impact are a function of their ability to be accurately and appropriately found, re-used, and cited over time by all stakeholders, both human and mechanical.

nodes.text: With this goal in mind, a workshop was held in Leiden, Netherlands, in 2014, named 'Jointly Designing a Data Fairport'. This workshop brought together a wide group of academic and private stakeholders all interested in overcoming data discovery and reuse obstacles. From the deliberations the notion emerged that, through the definition of and widespread support for a minimal set of community-agreed guiding principles, all stakeholders could more easily discover, access, integrate and re-use, and adequately cite the vast quantities of information being generated by data-intensive science. The meeting concluded with a draft formulation of foundational principles subsequently elaborated in greater detail—that all research objects should be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) both for machines and for people. A dedicated FAIR working group established by members of the FORCE11 community fine-tuned and improved the Principles.

nodes.text: Humans and machines often face distinct barriers when attempting to find and process data. Humans have an intuitive sense of 'semantics' because we can identify and interpret a wide variety of contextual cues, whether structural, visual, or narrative. We are less likely to make errors in selecting appropriate data, though humans face similar difficulties if sufficient metadata is lacking. The primary limitation of humans is that we are unable to operate at the scope, scale, and speed required by contemporary scientific data and e-Science complexity. Humans increasingly rely on computational agents to undertake discovery and integration tasks. This necessitates machines to be capable of autonomously and appropriately acting when faced with the wide range of types, formats, and access-mechanisms encountered during self-guided exploration of the global data ecosystem. It also necessitates that machines keep an exquisite record of provenance so that data can be accurately and adequately cited. Assisting these agents is a critical consideration for all participants in data management—from researchers and data producers to repository hosts.

nodes.text: Throughout the FAIR framework, 'machine actionable' indicates a continuum of possible states wherein a digital object provides increasingly more detailed information to an autonomously-acting computational data explorer. This information enables the agent—to a degree dependent on the amount of detail provided—to identify the type of object, determine if it is useful within the agent's current task by interrogating metadata and data elements, determine if it is usable with respect to license and accessibility constraints, and take appropriate action. For example, a machine may determine the data-type of a discovered digital object but not parse it due to unknown format; or it may process the contained data but not determine licensing requirements. The optimal state—where machines fully 'understand' and can autonomously and correctly operate on a digital object—may rarely be achieved. Nevertheless, the FAIR principles provide 'steps along a path' toward machine-actionability; adopting FAIR principles, in whole or in part, leads the resource along the continuum towards this optimal state. The idea of being machine-actionable applies in two contexts: the contextual metadata surrounding a digital object ('what is it?'), and the content of the digital object itself ('how do I process it/integrate it?'). Either, or both, may be machine-actionable, each forming its own continuum. A distinction exists between data that is machine-actionable as a result of specific investment in software supporting that data-type—bespoke parsers for life science or space science files—and data that is machine-actionable exclusively through general-purpose, open technologies. Ultimate machine-actionability occurs when a machine can make a useful decision regarding data it has not encountered before. This distinction is important when considering the rapidly growing data environment with new technologies and complex data-types continuously being developed, and the growth of general-purpose repositories where the data-types an agent encounters are unpredictable. Creating bespoke parsers in all computer languages for all data-types and analytical tools is not a sustainable activity. The focus on assisting machines in discovery and exploration of data through application of more generalized interoperability technologies and standards at the data/repository level becomes a first-priority for good data stewardship.

nodes.text: Representatives of interested stakeholder-groups coalesced around four core desiderata—the FAIR Guiding Principles. The FAIR Guiding Principles describe distinct considerations for contemporary data publishing environments with respect to supporting both manual and automated deposition, exploration, sharing, and reuse. While there have been numerous domain-focused publications advocating for specific improvements in data management and archival practices, FAIR differs in that it describes concise, domain-independent, high-level principles applicable to a wide range of scholarly outputs. Throughout the Principles, the phrase '(meta)data' is used where the Principle should be applied to both metadata and data. The elements of the FAIR Principles are related but independent and separable. They define characteristics that contemporary data resources, tools, vocabularies and infrastructures should exhibit to assist discovery and reuse. By minimally defining each guiding principle, the barrier-to-entry for data producers, publishers and stewards is purposely maintained as low as possible. The Principles may be adhered to in any combination and incrementally as data providers' publishing environments evolve to increasing degrees of 'FAIRness'. The modularity of the Principles and their distinction between data and metadata explicitly support a wide range of special circumstances. One such example is highly sensitive or personally-identifiable data, where publication of rich metadata to facilitate discovery, including clear rules regarding data access, provides a high degree of 'FAIRness' even in the absence of FAIR publication of the data itself. A second example involves the publication of non-data research objects. Analytical workflows are a critical component of the scholarly ecosystem, and their formal publication is necessary to achieve transparency and reproducibility. The FAIR principles can equally be applied to these non-data assets.

nodes.text: To be Findable: (meta)data are assigned globally unique and persistent identifiers; data are described with rich metadata; metadata clearly and explicitly include the identifier of the data it describes; (meta)data are registered or indexed in searchable resources. To be Accessible: (meta)data are retrievable by their identifier using standardized communications protocols; the protocol is open, free, and universally implementable; the protocol allows for authentication and authorization procedures where necessary; metadata remain accessible even when data are no longer available. To be Interoperable: (meta)data use a formal, accessible, shared, and broadly applicable language for knowledge representation; (meta)data use vocabularies that follow FAIR principles; (meta)data include qualified references to other (meta)data. To be Reusable: (meta)data are richly described with a plurality of accurate and relevant attributes; (meta)data are released with a clear and accessible data usage license; (meta)data are associated with detailed provenance; (meta)data meet domain-relevant community standards.

nodes.text: These high-level FAIR Guiding Principles precede implementation choices and do not suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution. They are not themselves a standard or specification. They act as a guide to data publishers and stewards in evaluating whether their implementation choices render their digital research artefacts Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable. The Principles enable a broad range of integrative and exploratory behaviours based on a wide range of technology choices and implementations.

nodes.text: Many repositories are already implementing various aspects of FAIR. Dataverse is an open-source data repository software installed in dozens of institutions globally. Harvard Dataverse, with more than 60,000 datasets, is the largest current instance and is open to all researchers from all fields. Dataverse generates a formal citation for each deposit and makes the Digital Object Identifier (DOI) or other persistent identifiers public when datasets are published, resolving to a landing page providing access to metadata, data files, terms, licenses, and version information. Metadata is always public even if data are restricted or removed for privacy, and is offered at three levels: data citation metadata, domain-specific metadata following standards within scientific domains, and file-level metadata including column-level information. Dataverse provides public machine-accessible interfaces to search data, access metadata and download files, using tokens to grant access when files are restricted.

nodes.text: FAIRDOM integrates the SEEK and openBIS platforms for FAIR data and model management. Individual research assets are identified with unique and persistent HTTP URLs, registrable with DOIs. Assets can be accessed over the Web in formats appropriate for individuals and machines (RDF, XML). Research assets are annotated with rich metadata using community standards, formats and ontologies, stored as RDF to enable interoperability.

nodes.text: ISA is a community-driven metadata tracking framework for standards-compliant collection, curation, management and reuse of life science datasets. ISA provides progressively FAIR structured metadata and underpins the EBI MetaboLights database. At its heart is a general-purpose, extensible ISA model, enhanced as RDF-based representations and JSON serializations to enable the 'I' and 'R' principles, becoming fully 'FAIR' when published as linked data.

nodes.text: Open PHACTS is a data integration platform for drug discovery information. Access is mediated through a machine-accessible interface providing multiple representations—human (HTML) and machine readable (RDF, JSON, XML, CSV)—providing the 'A' facet of FAIRness. The interface allows multiple URLs to access information about particular entities through mappings. A user can provide a ChEMBL URL to retrieve information from Chemspider or DrugBank; each call provides a canonical URL in its response. All data sources are described using standardized dataset descriptions following the global VoID standard with rich provenance, and all interface features are described using RDF following the Linked Data API specification.

nodes.text: wwPDB is a special-purpose, intensively-curated archive hosting information about experimentally-determined 3D structures of proteins and nucleic acids. All entries are stably hosted and represented in machine-readable formats (text and XML); the latter are machine-actionable using metadata conforming to the Macromolecular Information Framework (mmCIF), a data standard of the International Union of Crystallography. The metadata contains cross-references to common identifiers such as PubMed and NCBI Taxonomy and conforms to standards for chemical and structural biology domains. Software tools are available to interpret both wwPDB data and metadata. Each entry is represented by a DOI; other access points provide stable long-term URLs and all data and metadata are searchable through wwPDB-affiliated websites.

nodes.text: UniProt is a comprehensive resource for protein sequence and annotation data. All entries are uniquely identified by a stable URL providing access in multiple formats including web page, plain-text, and RDF. The record contains rich metadata both human-readable (HTML) and machine-readable (text and RDF), where the RDF response utilizes shared vocabularies and ontologies such as UniProt Core, FALDO, and ECO. Interlinking with more than 150 databases, every UniProt record has extensive links into PubMed, enabling rich citation. These links are machine-actionable in the RDF representation. The UniProt Core Ontology explicitly types all records, leaving no ambiguity about what the data represents, enabling fully-automated retrieval and cross-referencing.

nodes.text: The ideas within the FAIR Guiding Principles reflect, combine, and extend previous work by the Concept Web Alliance, who focused on machine-actionability and harmonization of data structures and semantics, and by organizations that developed the Joint Declaration of Data Citation Principles, who focused on making scholarly data citable, discoverable and available for reuse to support more rigorous scholarship. The FAIR Principles are also complementary to the 'Data Seal of Approval' in that they share the general aim to render data re-usable for users other than those who originally generated them. While the DSA focuses primarily on the responsibilities of data producers and repositories, FAIR focuses primarily on the data itself. Clearly, the broader community of stakeholders is coalescing around a set of common, dovetailed visions spanning all facets of the scholarly data publishing ecosystem. The end result, when implemented, will be more rigorous management and stewardship of these valuable digital resources, to the benefit of the entire academic community.

nodes.text: Good data management and stewardship is not a goal in itself, but rather a pre-condition supporting knowledge discovery and innovation. Contemporary e-Science requires data to be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable in the long-term, and these objectives are rapidly becoming expectations of agencies and publishers. The FAIR Data Principles provide a set of mileposts for data producers and publishers, guiding the implementation of the most basic levels of good Data Management and Stewardship practice. We call on all data producers and publishers to examine and implement these principles and actively participate with the FAIR initiative. By working together towards shared, common goals, the valuable data produced by our community will gradually achieve the critical goals of FAIRness.

nodes.text:
The FAIR Guiding Principles for Scientific Data Management and Stewardship (excerpt)

Abstract

There is an urgent need to improve the infrastructure supporting the reuse of scholarly data. A diverse set of stakeholders—representing academia, industry, funding agencies, and scholarly publishers—have come together to design and jointly endorse a concise and measureable set of principles that we refer to as the FAIR Data Principles. The intent is that these may act as a guideline for those wishing to enhance the reusability of their data holdings. Distinct from peer initiatives that focus on the human scholar, the FAIR Principles put specific emphasis on enhancing the ability of machines to automatically find and use the data, in addition to supporting its reuse by individuals. This Comment is the first formal publication of the FAIR Principles, and includes the rationale behind them, and some exemplar implementations in the community.
Comment

Supporting discovery through good data management

nodes.text: Good data management is not a goal in itself, but rather is the key conduit leading to knowledge discovery and innovation, and to subsequent data and knowledge integration and reuse by the community after the data publication process. Unfortunately, the existing digital ecosystem surrounding scholarly data publication prevents us from extracting maximum benefit from our research investments (e. g. , ref. 1 ). Partially in response to this, science funders, publishers and governmental agencies are beginning to require data management and stewardship plans for data generated in publicly funded experiments. Beyond proper collection, annotation, and archival, data stewardship includes the notion of ‘long-term care’ of valuable digital assets, with the goal that they should be discovered and re-used for downstream investigations, either alone, or in combination with newly generated data. The outcomes from good data management and stewardship, therefore, are high quality digital publications that facilitate and simplify this ongoing process of discovery, evaluation, and reuse in downstream studies. What constitutes ‘good data management’ is, however, largely undefined, and is generally left as a decision for the data or repository owner. Therefore, bringing some clarity around the goals and desiderata of good data management and stewardship, and defining simple guideposts to inform those who publish and/or preserve scholarly data, would be of great utility. This article describes four foundational principles—Findability, Accessibility, Interoperability, and Reusability—that serve to guide data producers and publishers as they navigate around these obstacles, thereby helping to maximize the added-value gained by contemporary, formal scholarly digital publishing. Importantly, it is our intent that the principles apply not only to ‘data’ in the conventional sense, but also to the algorithms, tools, and workflows that led to that data. All scholarly digital research objects—from data to analytical pipelines—benefit from application of these principles, since all components of the research process must be available to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and reusability.

nodes.text: There are numerous and diverse stakeholders who stand to benefit from overcoming these obstacles: researchers wanting to share, get credit, and reuse each other’s data and interpretations; professional data publishers offering their services; software and tool-builders providing data analysis and processing services such as reusable workflows; funding agencies (private and public) increasingly concerned with long-term data stewardship; and a data science community mining, integrating and analysing new and existing data to advance discovery. To facilitate the reading of this manuscript by these diverse stakeholders, we provide definitions for common abbreviations in Box 1 . Humans, however, are not the only critical stakeholders in the milieu of scientific data. Similar problems are encountered by the applications and computational agents that we task to undertake data retrieval and analysis on our behalf. These ‘computational stakeholders’ are increasingly relevant, and demand as much, or more, attention as their importance grows. One of the grand challenges of data-intensive science, therefore, is to improve knowledge discovery through assisting both humans, and their computational agents, in the discovery of, access to, and integration and analysis of, task-appropriate scientific data and other scholarly digital objects. For certain types of important digital objects, there are well-curated, deeply-integrated, special-purpose repositories such as Genbank, Worldwide Protein Data Bank (wwPDB), and UniProt in the life sciences; Space Physics Data Facility (SPDF; http://spdf. gsfc. nasa. gov/ ) and Set of Identifications, Measurements and Bibliography for Astronomical Data (SIMBAD) in the space sciences. These foundational and critical core resources are continuously curating and capturing high-value reference datasets and fine-tuning them to enhance scholarly output, provide support for both human and mechanical users, and provide extensive tooling to access their content in rich, dynamic ways. However, not all datasets or even data types can be captured by, or submitted to, these repositories.

nodes.text: Many important datasets emerging from traditional, low-throughput bench science don’t fit in the data models of these special-purpose repositories, yet these datasets are no less important with respect to integrative research, reproducibility, and reuse in general. Apparently in response to this, we see the emergence of numerous general-purpose data repositories, at scales ranging from institutional (for example, a single university), to open globally-scoped repositories such as Dataverse, FigShare ( http://figshare. com ), Dryad, Mendeley Data ( https://data. mendeley. com/ ), Zenodo ( http://zenodo. org/ ), DataHub ( http://datahub. io ), DANS ( http://www. dans. knaw. nl/ ), and EUDat. Such repositories accept a wide range of data types in a wide variety of formats, generally do not attempt to integrate or harmonize the deposited data, and place few restrictions (or requirements) on the descriptors of the data deposition. The resulting data ecosystem, therefore, appears to be moving away from centralization, is becoming more diverse, and less integrated, thereby exacerbating the discovery and re-usability problem for both human and computational stakeholders. A specific example of these obstacles could be imagined in the domain of gene regulation and expression analysis. Suppose a researcher has generated a dataset of differentially-selected polyadenylation sites in a non-model pathogenic organism grown under a variety of environmental conditions that stimulate its pathogenic state. The researcher is interested in comparing the alternatively-polyadenylated genes in this local dataset, to other examples of alternative-polyadenylation, and the expression levels of these genes—both in this organism and related model organisms—during the infection process. Given that there is no special-purpose archive for differential polyadenylation data, and no model organism database for this pathogen, where does the researcher begin? We will consider the current approach to this problem from a variety of data discovery and integration perspectives. If the desired datasets existed, where might they have been published, and how would one begin to search for them, using what search tools?

nodes.text: The desired search would need to filter based on specific species, specific tissues, specific types of data (Poly-A, microarray, NGS), specific conditions (infection), and specific genes—is that information (‘metadata’) captured by the repositories, and if so, what formats is it in, is it searchable, and how? Once the data is discovered, can it be downloaded? In what format(s)? Can that format be easily integrated with private in-house data (the local dataset of alternative polyadenylation sites) as well as other data publications from third-parties and with the community’s core gene/protein data repositories? Can this integration be done automatically to save time and avoid copy/paste errors? Does the researcher have permission to use the data from these third-party researchers, under what license conditions, and who should be cited if a data-point is re-used? Questions such as these highlight some of the barriers to data discovery and reuse, not only for humans, but even more so for machines; yet it is precisely these kinds of deeply and broadly integrative analyses that constitute the bulk of contemporary e-Science. The reason that we often need several weeks (or months) of specialist technical effort to gather the data necessary to answer such research questions is not the lack of appropriate technology; the reason is, that we do not pay our valuable digital objects the careful attention they deserve when we create and preserve them. Overcoming these barriers, therefore, necessitates that all stakeholders—including researchers, special-purpose, and general-purpose repositories—evolve to meet the emergent challenges described above. The goal is for scholarly digital objects of all kinds to become ‘first class citizens’ in the scientific publication ecosystem, where the quality of the publication—and more importantly, the impact of the publication—is a function of its ability to be accurately and appropriately found, re-used, and cited over time, by all stakeholders, both human and mechanical.

nodes.text: With this goal in-mind, a workshop was held in Leiden, Netherlands, in 2014, named ‘Jointly Designing a Data Fairport’. This workshop brought together a wide group of academic and private stakeholders all of whom had an interest in overcoming data discovery and reuse obstacles. From the deliberations at the workshop the notion emerged that, through the definition of, and widespread support for, a minimal set of community-agreed guiding principles and practices, all stakeholders could more easily discover, access, appropriately integrate and re-use, and adequately cite, the vast quantities of information being generated by contemporary data-intensive science. The meeting concluded with a draft formulation of a set of foundational principles that were subsequently elaborated in greater detail—namely, that all research objects should be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) both for machines and for people. These are now referred to as the FAIR Guiding Principles. Subsequently, a dedicated FAIR working group, established by several members of the FORCE11 community fine-tuned and improved the Principles. The results of these efforts are reported here. Box 1: Terms and Abbreviations

Terms and Abbreviations

nodes.text:
BD2K —Big Data 2 Knowledge, is a trans-NIH initiative established to enable biomedical research as a digital research enterprise, to facilitate discovery and support new knowledge, and to maximise community engagement.
DOI —Digital Object Identifier; a code used to permanently and stably identify (usually digital) objects. DOIs provide a standard mechanism for retrieval of metadata about the object, and generally a means to access the data object itself.
FAIR —Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable.
FORCE11 —The Future of Research Communications and e-Scholarship; a community of scholars, librarians, archivists, publishers and research funders that has arisen organically to help facilitate the change toward improved knowledge creation and sharing, initiated in 2011.
Interoperability —the ability of data or tools from non-cooperating resources to integrate or work together with minimal effort.
JDDCP —Joint Declaration of Data Citation Principles; Acknowledging data as a first-class research output, and to support good research practices around data re-use, JDDCP proposes a set of guiding principles for citation of data within scholarly literature, another dataset, or any other research object.
RDF —Resource Description Framework; a globally-accepted framework for data and knowledge representation that is intended to be read and interpreted by machines.

nodes.text: The emphasis placed on FAIRness being applied to both human-driven and machine-driven activities, is a specific focus of the FAIR Guiding Principles that distinguishes them from many peer initiatives (discussed in the subsequent section). Humans and machines often face distinct barriers when attempting to find and process data on the Web. Humans have an intuitive sense of ‘semantics’ (the meaning or intent of a digital object) because we are capable of identifying and interpreting a wide variety of contextual cues, whether those take the form of structural/visual/iconic cues in the layout of a Web page, or the content of narrative notes. As such, we are less likely to make errors in the selection of appropriate data or other digital objects, although humans will face similar difficulties if sufficient contextual metadata is lacking. The primary limitation of humans, however, is that we are unable to operate at the scope, scale, and speed necessitated by the scale of contemporary scientific data and complexity of e-Science. It is for this reason that humans increasingly rely on computational agents to undertake discovery and integration tasks on their behalf. This necessitates machines to be capable of autonomously and appropriately acting when faced with the wide range of types, formats, and access-mechanisms/protocols that will be encountered during their self-guided exploration of the global data ecosystem. It also necessitates that the machines keep an exquisite record of provenance such that the data they are collecting can be accurately and adequately cited. Assisting these agents, therefore, is a critical consideration for all participants in the data management and stewardship process—from researchers and data producers to data repository hosts. Throughout this paper, we use the phrase ‘machine actionable’ to indicate a continuum of possible states wherein a digital object provides increasingly more detailed information to an autonomously-acting, computational data explorer.

nodes.text: This information enables the agent—to a degree dependent on the amount of detail provided—to have the capacity, when faced with a digital object never encountered before, to: a) identify the type of object (with respect to both structure and intent), b) determine if it is useful within the context of the agent’s current task by interrogating metadata and/or data elements, c) determine if it is usable, with respect to license, consent, or other accessibility or use constraints, and d) take appropriate action, in much the same manner that a human would. For example, a machine may be capable of determining the data-type of a discovered digital object, but not capable of parsing it due to it being in an unknown format; or it may be capable of processing the contained data, but not capable of determining the licensing requirements related to the retrieval and/or use of that data. The optimal state—where machines fully ‘understand’ and can autonomously and correctly operate-on a digital object—may rarely be achieved. Nevertheless, the FAIR principles provide ‘steps along a path’ toward machine-actionability; adopting, in whole or in part, the FAIR principles, leads the resource along the continuum towards this optimal state. In addition, the idea of being machine-actionable applies in two contexts—first, when referring to the contextual metadata surrounding a digital object (‘what is it? ’), and second, when referring to the content of the digital object itself (‘how do I process it/integrate it? ’). Either, or both of these may be machine-actionable, and each forms its own continuum of actionability. Finally, we wish to draw a distinction between data that is machine-actionable as a result of specific investment in software supporting that data-type, for example, bespoke parsers that understand life science wwPDB files or space science Space Physics Archive Search and Extract (SPASE) files, and data that is machine-actionable exclusively through the utilization of general-purpose, open technologies.

nodes.text: To reiterate the earlier point—ultimate machine-actionability occurs when a machine can make a useful decision regarding data that it has not encountered before. This distinction is important when considering both (a) the rapidly growing and evolving data environment, with new technologies and new, more complex data-types continuously being developed, and (b) the growth of general-purpose repositories, where the data-types likely to be encountered by an agent are unpredictable. Creating bespoke parsers, in all computer languages, for all data-types and all analytical tools that require those data-types, is not a sustainable activity. As such, the focus on assisting machines in their discovery and exploration of data through application of more generalized interoperability technologies and standards at the data/repository level, becomes a first-priority for good data stewardship. nodes.text: Representatives of the interested stakeholder-groups, discussed above, coalesced around four core desiderata—the FAIR Guiding Principles—and limited elaboration of these, which have been refined ( Box 2 ) from the meeting’s original draft, available at ( https://www. force11. org/node/6062 ). A separate document that dynamically addresses community discussion relating to clarifications and explanations of the principles, and detailed guidelines for and examples of FAIR implementations, is currently being constructed ( http://datafairport. org/fair-principles-living-document-menu ). The FAIR Guiding Principles describe distinct considerations for contemporary data publishing environments with respect to supporting both manual and automated deposition, exploration, sharing, and reuse. While there have been a number of recent, often domain-focused publications advocating for specific improvements in practices relating to data management and archival, FAIR differs in that it describes concise, domain-independent, high-level principles that can be applied to a wide range of scholarly outputs. Throughout the Principles, we use the phrase ‘(meta)data’ in cases where the Principle should be applied to both metadata and data. The elements of the FAIR Principles are related, but independent and separable. The Principles define characteristics that contemporary data resources, tools, vocabularies and infrastructures should exhibit to assist discovery and reuse by third-parties. By minimally defining each guiding principle, the barrier-to-entry for data producers, publishers and stewards who wish to make their data holdings FAIR is purposely maintained as low as possible. The Principles may be adhered to in any combination and incrementally, as data providers’ publishing environments evolve to increasing degrees of ‘FAIRness’. Moreover, the modularity of the Principles, and their distinction between data and metadata, explicitly support a wide range of special circumstances. One such example is highly sensitive or personally-identifiable data, where publication of rich metadata to facilitate discovery, including clear rules regarding the process for accessing the data, provides a high degree of ‘FAIRness’ even in the absence of FAIR publication of the data itself.

nodes.text: A second example involves the publication of non-data research objects. Analytical workflows, for example, are a critical component of the scholarly ecosystem, and their formal publication is necessary to achieve both transparency and scientific reproducibility. The FAIR principles can equally be applied to these non-data assets, which need to be identified, described, discovered, and reused in much the same manner as data. Specific exemplar efforts that provide varying levels of FAIRness are detailed later in this document. Additional issues, however, remain to be addressed. First, when community-endorsed vocabularies or other (meta)data standards do not include the attributes necessary to achieve rich annotation, there are two possible solutions: either publish an extension of an existing, closely related vocabulary, or—in the extreme case—create and explicitly publish a new vocabulary resource, following FAIR principles (‘I2’). Second, to explicitly identify the standard chosen when more than one vocabulary or other (meta)data standard is available, and given that for instance in the life sciences there are over 600 content standards, the BioSharing registry ( https://biosharing. org/ ) can be of use as it describes the standards in detail, including versions where applicable. Box 2: The FAIR Guiding Principles

nodes.text:
To be Findable:
F1. (meta)data are assigned a globally unique and persistent identifier
F2. data are described with rich metadata (defined by R1 below)
F3. metadata clearly and explicitly include the identifier of the data it describes
F4. (meta)data are registered or indexed in a searchable resource
To be Accessible:
A1. (meta)data are retrievable by their identifier using a standardized communications protocol
A1.1 the protocol is open, free, and universally implementable
A1.2 the protocol allows for an authentication and authorization procedure, where necessary
A2. metadata are accessible, even when the data are no longer available
To be Interoperable:
I1. (meta)data use a formal, accessible, shared, and broadly applicable language for knowledge representation.
I2. (meta)data use vocabularies that follow FAIR principles
I3. (meta)data include qualified references to other (meta)data
To be Reusable:
R1. meta(data) are richly described with a plurality of accurate and relevant attributes
R1.1. (meta)data are released with a clear and accessible data usage license
R1.2. (meta)data are associated with detailed provenance
R1.3. (meta)data meet domain-relevant community standards
The Principles precede implementation

These high-level FAIR Guiding Principles precede implementation choices, and do not suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution; moreover, the Principles are not, themselves, a standard or a specification. They act as a guide to data publishers and stewards to assist them in evaluating whether their particular implementation choices are rendering their digital research artefacts Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable. We anticipate that these high level principles will enable a broad range of integrative and exploratory behaviours, based on a wide range of technology choices and implementations. Indeed, many repositories are already implementing various aspects of FAIR using a variety of technology choices and several examples are detailed in the next section; examples include Scientific Data itself and how narrative data articles are anchored to a progressively FAIR structured metadata.

nodes.text: Dataverse : Dataverse is an open-source data repository software installed in dozens of institutions globally to support public community repositories or institutional research data repositories. Harvard Dataverse, with more than 60,000 datasets, is the largest of the current Dataverse repositories, and is open to all researchers from all research fields. Dataverse generates a formal citation for each deposit, following the standard defined by Altman and King. Dataverse makes the Digital Object Identifier (DOI), or other persistent identifiers (Handles), public when the dataset is published (‘F’). This resolves to a landing page, providing access to metadata, data files, dataset terms, waivers or licenses, and version information, all of which is indexed and searchable (‘F’, ‘A’, and ‘R’). Deposits include metadata, data files, and any complementary files (such as documentation or code) needed to understand the data and analysis (‘R’). Metadata is always public, even if the data are restricted or removed for privacy issues (‘F’, ‘A’). This metadata is offered at three levels, extensively supporting the ‘I’ and ‘R’ FAIR principles: 1) data citation metadata, which maps to DataCite schema or Dublin Core Terms, 2) domain-specific metadata, which when possible maps to metadata standards used within a scientific domain, and 3) file-level metadata, which can be deep and extensive for tabular data files (including column-level metadata). Finally, Dataverse provides public machine-accessible interfaces to search the data, access the metadata and download the data files, using a token to grant access when data files are restricted (‘A’). FAIRDOM ( http://fair-dom. org/about ): integrates the SEEK and openBIS platforms to produce a FAIR data and model management facility for Systems Biology. Individual research assets (or aggregates of data and models) are identified with unique and persistent HTTP URLs, which can be registered with DOIs for publication (‘F’). Assets can be accessed over the Web in a variety of formats appropriate for individuals and/or their computers (RDF, XML) (‘I’).

nodes.text: Research assets are annotated with rich metadata, using community standards, formats and ontologies (‘I’). The metadata is stored as RDF to enable interoperability and assets can be downloaded for reuse (‘R’). ISA : is a community-driven metadata tracking framework to facilitate standards-compliant collection, curation, management and reuse of life science datasets. ISA provides progressively FAIR structured metadata to Nature Scientific Data’s Data Descriptor articles, and many GigaScience data papers, and underpins the EBI MetaboLights database among other data resources. At the heart is a general-purpose, extensible ISA model, originally only available as a tabular representation but subsequently enhanced as an RDF-based representation, and JSON serializations to enable the ‘I’ and ‘R’, becoming ‘FAIR’ when published as linked data ( http://elixir-uk. org/node-events/201cisa-as-a-fair-research-object201d-hack-the-spec-event-1 ) and complementing other research objects. Open PHACTS : Open PHACTS is a data integration platform for information pertaining to drug discovery. Access to the platform is mediated through a machine-accessible interface which provides multiple representations that are both human (HTML) and machine readable (RDF, JSON, XML, CSV, etc), providing the ‘A’ facet of FAIRness. The interface allows multiple URLs to be used to access information about a particular entity through a mappings service (‘F’ and ‘A’). Thus, a user can provide a ChEMBL URL to retrieve information sourced from, for example, Chemspider or DrugBank. Each call provides a canonical URL in its response (‘A’ and ‘I’). All data sources used are described using standardized dataset descriptions, following the global VoID standard, with rich provenance (‘R’ and ‘I’). All interface features are described using RDF following the Linked Data API specification (‘A’). Finally, a majority of the datasets are described using community agreed upon ontologies (‘I’). wwPDB : wwPDB is a special-purpose, intensively-curated data archive that hosts information about experimentally-determined 3D structures of proteins and nucleic acids.

nodes.text: All wwPDB entries are stably hosted on an FTP server (‘A’) and represented in machine-readable formats (text and XML); the latter are machine-actionable using the metadata provided by the wwPDB conforming to the Macromolecular Information Framework (mmCIF), a data standard of the International Union of Crystallography (IUCr) (‘F’,‘I’ for humans, ‘F’,‘I’ for IUCr-aware machines). The wwPDB metadata contains cross-references to common identifiers such as PubMed and NCBI Taxonomy, and their wwPDB metadata are described in data dictionaries and schema documents ( http://mmcif. wwpdb. org and http://pdbml. wwpdb. org ) which conform to the IUCr data standard for the chemical and structural biology domains (‘R’). A variety of software tools are available to interpret both wwPDB data and meta-data (‘I’,‘R’ for humans, ‘I’,‘R’ for machines with this software). Each entry is represented by a DOI (‘F’, ‘A’ for humans and machines). The DOI resolves to a zipped file which requires special software for further interrogation/interpretation. Other wwPDB access points provide access to wwPDB records through URLs that are likely to be stable in the long-term (‘F’), and all data and metadata is searchable through one or more of the wwPDB-affiliated websites (‘F’) UniProt : UniProt is a comprehensive resource for protein sequence and annotation data. All entries are uniquely identified by a stable URL, that provides access to the record in a variety of formats including a web page, plain-text, and RDF (‘F’ and ‘A’). The record contains rich metadata (‘F’) that is both human-readable (HTML) and machine-readable (text and RDF), where the RDF formatted response utilizes shared vocabularies and ontologies such as UniProt Core, FALDO, and ECO (‘I’). Interlinking with more than 150 different databases, every UniProt record has extensive links into, for example, PubMed, enabling rich citation. These links are machine-actionable in the RDF representation (‘R’).

nodes.text: Finally, in the RDF representation, the UniProt Core Ontology explicitly types all records, leaving no ambiguity—neither for humans nor machines—about what the data represents (‘R’), enabling fully-automated retrieval of records and cross-referencing information. In addition to, and in support of, communities and resources that are already pursuing FAIR objectives, the Data Citation Implementation Group of Force11 has published specific technical recommendations for how to implement many of the principles, with a particular focus on identifiers and their resolution, persistence, and metadata accessibility especially related to citation. In addition, the ‘Skunkworks’ group that emerged from the Lorentz Workshop has been creating software supporting infrastructures that are, end-to-end, compatible with FAIR principles, and can be implemented over existing repositories. These code modules have a particular focus on metadata publication and searchability, compatibility in cases of strict privacy considerations, and the extremely difficult problem of data and metadata interoperability (manuscript in preparation). Finally, there are several emergent projects, some listed in Box 3 , for which FAIR is a key objective. These projects may provide valuable advice and guidance for those wishing to become more FAIR. FAIRness is a prerequisite for proper data management and data stewardship

nodes.text: The ideas within the FAIR Guiding Principles reflect, combine, build upon and extend previous work by both the Concept Web Alliance ( https://conceptweblog. wordpress. com/ ) partners, who focused on machine-actionability and harmonization of data structures and semantics, and by the scientific and scholarly organizations that developed the Joint Declaration of Data Citation Principles (JDDCP), who focused on primary scholarly data being made citable, discoverable and available for reuse, so as to be capable of supporting more rigorous scholarship. An attempt to define the similarities and overlaps between the FAIR Principles and the JDDCP is provided at ( https://www. force11. org/node/6062 ). The FAIR Principles are also complementary to the ‘Data Seal of Approval’ (DSA) ( http://datasealofapproval. org/media/filer_public/2013/09/27/guidelines_2014-2015. pdf ) in that they share the general aim to render data re-usable for users other than those who originally generated them. While the DSA focuses primarily on the responsibilities and conduct of data producers and repositories, FAIR focuses primarily on the data itself. Clearly, the broader community of stakeholders is coalescing around a set of common, dovetailed visions spanning all facets of the scholarly data publishing ecosystem. The end result, when implemented, will be more rigorous management and stewardship of these valuable digital resources, to the benefit of the entire academic community. As stated at the outset, good data management and stewardship is not a goal in itself, but rather a pre-condition supporting knowledge discovery and innovation. Contemporary e-Science requires data to be Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable in the long-term, and these objectives are rapidly becoming expectations of agencies and publishers. We demonstrate, therefore, that the FAIR Data Principles provide a set of mileposts for data producers and publishers. They guide the implementation of the most basic levels of good Data Management and Stewardship practice, thus helping researchers adhere to the expectations and requirements of their funding agencies.

We call on all data producers and publishers to examine and implement these principles, and actively participate with the FAIR initiative by joining the Force11 working group. By working together towards shared, common goals, the valuable data produced by our community will gradually achieve the critical goals of FAIRness. Additional Information
